content.full_text:
memorandum

: File                                                    Date  : 9-1-77

                                                          M-99-437

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Mike Baker

Subject: Telephone conversation with Bill Leach August 15, 1977.


On August 11, Bill Leach, attorney for TRW (213-536-1282) called
to ask questions concerning the lobbyist regulations. I called
him back on August 15.

TRW has an employee that follows legislation for the corporation.
Mr. Leach asked whether certain activities would make that employee
a lobbyist:

     (1) If the employee engages in no direct communication,
     can he spend an unlimited amount of work doing support
     activity?

     I told him the answer was yes.

     (2) If the employee engages in more than 10 hours of direct
     communication within two consecutive months, what activity
     do you look to in order to determine whether the 40-hour
     threshold is met? Do you look only at activity related to
     the bills or actions which were the subject of the employee's
     direct communication, or do you look at all time spent re-
     lated to "influencing legislative or administrative action?"

     I responded that under the Commission's regulation (2 Cal.
     Adm. Code Section 18239) certain activities listed for re-
     search were not includable. In addition, I read him the com-
     ment respecting subsection (d) in which it is indicated that
     lobbying activities are said to include "monitoring bills
     and regulations which one is attempting to influence,...
     attending hearings and floor debates on bills and regulations
     which one is attempting to influence." I told him that the
     language suggested that the activity to reach the 40-hour
     threshold should be related to the activity which is the
     subject of the employee's direct communication. I indicated,
     however, that meeting the 10-hour direct communication
     threshold is the key element, and that once that threshold
     is met, a broad view should be taken of other activities so

File
9-1-77
2

that all those which might be related to the direct com-
munication are included to meet the 40-hour threshold.

(3) To meet the 10-hour direct communication threshold,
must all the 10 hours be spent discussing business?

I told him the answer was no. If two people discussed
a bill for half an hour and discussed baseball for 9-1/2
hours, whether or not during the same meeting, we would
still regard that as 10 hours of direct communication. I
explained that the theory behind that was that the contact
between lobbyist and legislator, no matter what the subject,
could possibly give rise to influence. I also indicated,
however, that if none of the 10 hours was spent discussing
legislative or administrative action, the 10-hour threshold
would not be met. I indicated that it was not the intent
of the law to make a person a lobbyist if he or she meets
socially with the legislator for 10 hours during a two-
month period and no business is discussed.

MJB:jo

Note to file:

These conclusions were discussed and agreed to at the opinion
request meeting on September 26, 1977.

sections.question_synthetic: When does an employee following legislation for a corporation become a lobbyist under California law, specifically regarding thresholds for direct communication hours, support activities, and what activities count toward the 40-hour threshold?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: An employee can engage in unlimited support activity without direct communication; the 10-hour direct communication threshold is the key element, and once met, a broad view of related activities applies to reach the 40-hour threshold; direct communication hours need not all involve business discussion but must include at least some discussion of legislative or administrative action.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is informal file memo of telephone conversation rather than formal advice letter; no explicit QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections but contains three numbered questions and corresponding answers from FPPC staff; conclusions were later confirmed at opinion request meeting on 9-26-77

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: When does an employee following legislation for a corporation become a lobbyist under California law, specifically regarding thresholds for direct communication hours, support activities, and what activities count toward the 40-hour threshold?

CONCLUSION: An employee can engage in unlimited support activity without direct communication; the 10-hour direct communication threshold is the key element, and once met, a broad view of related activities applies to reach the 40-hour threshold; direct communication hours need not all involve business discussion but must include at least some discussion of legislative or administrative action.

On August 11, Bill Leach, attorney for TRW (213-536-1282) called to ask questions concerning the lobbyist regulations. I called him back on August 15. TRW has an employee that follows legislation for the corporation. Mr. Leach asked whether certain activities would make that employee a lobbyist: (1) If the employee engages in no direct communication, can he spend an unlimited amount of work doing support activity? I told him the answer was yes. (2) If the employee engages in more than 10 hours of direct communication within two consecutive months, what activity do you look to in order to determine whether the 40-hour threshold is met? Do you look only at activity related to the bills or actions which were the subject of the employee's direct communication, or do you look at all time spent re- lated to "influencing legislative or administrative action?" I responded that under the Commission's regulation (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239) certain activities listed for re- search were not includable. In addition, I read him the com- ment respecting subsection (d) in which it is indicated that lobbying activities are said to include "monitoring bills and regulations which one is attempting to influence,... attending hearings and floor debates on bills and regulations which one is attempting to influence." I told him that the language suggested that the activity to reach the 40-hour threshold should be related to the activity which is the subject of the employee's direct communication. (3) To meet the 10-hour direct communication threshold, must all the 10 hours be spent discussing business? I told him the answer was no. If two people discussed a bill for half an hour and discussed baseball for 9-1/2 hours, whether or not during the same meeting, we would still regard that as 10 hours of direct communication. I explained that the theory behind that was that the contact between lobbyist and legislator, no matter what the subject, could possibly give rise to influence. I also indicated, however, that if none of the 10 hours was spent discussing legislative or administrative action, the 10-hour threshold would not be met. I indicated that it was not the intent of the law to make a person a lobbyist if he or she meets socially with the legislator for 10 hours during a two- month period and no business is discussed. embedding.summary: FPPC staff responded to TRW's attorney regarding when the corporation's legislative monitoring employee would be classified as a lobbyist under state regulations, addressing thresholds for direct communication, support activities, and how to measure lobbying hours.
